title: Coefficient of linear expansion
short: The fractional change in length per degree of temperature change, α, in units of 1/K.
full:
The coefficient of linear expansion α measures how much a material stretches per unit length for each degree of heating: ΔL = α·L₀·ΔT. Most solids expand when heated because the atoms vibrate more strongly and sit slightly farther apart.

The values are tiny (steel α ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻⁵ 1/K), but over long spans or large temperature swings they matter — hence expansion joints in bridges and railway tracks.

The SI unit is the reciprocal kelvin (1/K).
